prevalence.source: National Centre for Disease Control (NCDC) / NVBDCP-NCVBDC / WHO
prevalence.trend: increasing in many urban centers with expanding Aedes range and seasonal peaks

burden.mortalityRate: Low overall; higher in delayed presentation and severe dengue
burden.source: IHME Global Burden of Disease (GBD) / WHO

riskFactors: Aedes exposure in dense urban settings
monsoon season and water storage practices
limited access to early diagnostics in some districts
epidemiology.diagnosticCriteria: Clinical case definition; confirmation with NS1/RT-PCR (early) or IgM; severity classification per national dengue guidelines

publicHealthSystem.screeningCoverage: no screening; outbreak surveillance and fever clinic testing in many cities
publicHealthSystem.controlRate: Good outcomes with timely supportive care; severe dengue depends on early detection and referral

regionalSpecificities.diagnosisCriteria: NS1 and CBC commonly used in urban centers; limited access in rural areas leads to clinical diagnosis and referral
regionalSpecificities.treatmentProtocol: Supportive care with fluids guided by hematocrit and vitals; avoidance of NSAIDs; platelet transfusion only for specific indications
regionalSpecificities.regionalChallenges: Underreporting, variable lab access, and high seasonal caseloads in tertiary hospitals during monsoon peaks
regionalSpecificities.strongPoints: Growing fever-clinic capacity in cities and established national surveillance programmes for vector-borne disease control
